description: The provided image displays a publisher interface icon labeled "Check for updates" featuring a red bookmark symbol enclosed within a circular yellow-gray badge. This image does not constitute a scientific figure and contains no experimentally derived material science information such as scanning electron micrographs, X-ray diffraction patterns, spectroscopic data, or mechanical property curves pertaining to the Fe-9Mn-5Al-0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel processed via intercritical quenching-annealing. No phases, grain structures, precipitates, interfaces, or quantitative metrics including volume fractions, dislocation densities, or austenite stability parameters are discernible from the pixel data. The visual content appears to be a navigational user-interface element rather than an experimental sub-figure. Consequently, no specific sample conditions, annealing temperatures, processing parameters, or property trends can be visually verified. The host paper examines microstructure and mechanical property evolution across annealing temperatures of 620–800°C, yet this image offers no direct evidentiary support for those scientific conclusions.

This schematic temperature-time diagram illustrates the complete thermomechanical processing and heat treatment schedule for the experimental Fe-9Mn-5Al-0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel. The process route begins with homogenization at 1100 °C for 2 h, followed by hot rolling as the temperature decreases, and subsequent air cooling to room temperature to obtain the hot-rolled sample HR. The plate is then reheated to 1050 °C in the ferrite-austenite dual-phase region for 1 h and water quenched (sample IQ-1050). Finally, the quenched plate undergoes annealing for 1 h at various temperatures; the diagram explicitly marks 800 °C and 620 °C with reference to the Ac1 temperature of 617 °C shown as a dashed line, representing the intercritical quenching-annealing conditions that produce samples QA-620, QA-680, QA-740, and QA-800, before air cooling to room temperature. As the caption reports, hot rolling started near 1050 °C and finished near 950 °C. This figure defines the critical thermal processing window for evaluating the effects of annealing temperature on microstructure and mechanical properties.

Figure 2 presents Thermo-Calc equilibrium calculations for an Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density δ-TRIP steel (approximately Fe-8.9Mn-4.6Al-0.29C). Subfigure (a) plots the mole fraction of equilibrium phases—FCC_A1 (austenite), BCC_A2 (δ-ferrite), cementite, and liquid—as a function of temperature. δ-ferrite precipitates from liquid near 1485 °C, austenite forms via peritectic reaction around 1390 °C, reaches a maximum fraction of ~91.4 % at ~970 °C, and cementite appears at the A1 temperature of ~617 °C. Subfigure (b) shows the mass fraction of Mn, Al, and C partitioned between ferrite and austenite versus temperature: Mn and C in austenite increase strongly below 970 °C, whereas C in austenite drops near 617 °C due to cementite precipitation. These results justify the selection of 1050 °C as the intercritical quenching temperature and 620–800 °C as intercritical annealing temperatures, revealing that 4.6 % Al stabilizes δ-ferrite and prevents full austenitization.

This figure presents eight scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs illustrating the microstructural evolution of a low-density Fe-9Mn-5Al-0.3C δ-TRIP steel across different thermomechanical processing stages. Subfigures (a) and (b) show the hot-rolled (HR) state, revealing a laminated microstructure composed of δ-ferrite (δ-F) bands alternating with lath martensite (M), together with retained austenite (RA) and α-ferrite (α-F). Subfigures (c) and (d) display the intercritically quenched state at 1050 °C (IQ-1050), where the laminated morphology is inherited but δ-F bands become thinner and discontinuous due to prior austenite grain growth and prior austenite grain boundary (PAGB) migration. Subfigures (e) through (h) depict the quenching-annealed (QA) specimens at 620 °C, 680 °C, 740 °C, and 800 °C, showing tempered martensite with fine reverted austenite (A) films among laths. Notably, carbides (Cm) precipitate within tempered martensite only at 620 °C, while new martensite forms during air cooling at 800 °C (red box in h). The images collectively demonstrate that annealing temperature critically governs phase fractions, austenite stability, precipitation behavior, and the resulting mechanical properties.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) band contrast (BC) maps overlaid with austenite phase distribution (highlighted in red) for an Fe-9Mn-5Al-0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel under six processing conditions. Subfigures (a) through (f) correspond to the hot-rolled state (HR), intercritical quenching at 1050 °C (IQ-1050), and quenching-annealing at 620 °C, 680 °C, 740 °C, and 800 °C (QA-620 to QA-800), respectively. In the BC maps, bright white regions indicate ferrite due to high BC values, whereas darker gray regions represent lath martensite or tempered martensite associated with lower BC values. The HR specimen exhibits relatively concentrated austenite within martensite bands, while IQ-1050 shows very little austenite and coarser δ-ferrite bands segmented by martensite lamellae. For the QA series, fine austenite grains—including retained and reverted austenite—become increasingly dispersed within tempered martensite regions as the annealing temperature increases from 620 °C to 740 °C. At QA-800, the red-highlighted austenite fraction appears comparable to or slightly reduced compared with QA-740, consistent with partial martensite transformation during subsequent air cooling. The figure demonstrates that intercritical quenching-annealing produces substantially higher and more uniformly distributed austenite contents than the HR and IQ-1050 states, establishing the microstructural basis for the TRIP effect in this steel.

This figure presents the room-temperature mechanical behavior of a low-density Fe-9Mn-5Al-0.3C δ-TRIP steel processed under different heat treatment conditions. Panel (a) displays engineering stress-strain curves for the hot-rolled (HR), intercritically quenched (IQ-1050), and quenching-annealed (QA-620, QA-680, QA-740, QA-800) specimens. The HR sample exhibits the highest tensile strength (~1107 MPa) but poor ductility (~5.6%). After intercritical quenching, IQ-1050 shows increased yield strength (~575 MPa) yet reduced total elongation (~2.0%). The QA series demonstrates a strength-ductility trade-off with increasing annealing temperature: QA-620 achieves ~864 MPa tensile strength with ~44% elongation, while QA-740 attains the superior ductility of ~62.4% with ~725 MPa strength. Panel (b) shows work hardening rate (WHR) versus true strain for QA specimens. At ε < 0.01, WHR drops sharply due to soft ferrite deformation. For 0.01–0.1 true strain, QA-620 exhibits distinctly higher WHR than other QA conditions, attributed to carbide precipitation impeding dislocation glide. Beyond ε = 0.15, diverging WHR trends reflect varying TRIP effect intensities. These results indicate that annealing temperature critically governs austenite stability and deformation mechanisms, thereby optimizing the synergy of strength and ductility.

This figure comprises four scanning electron micrographs depicting the tensile fracture surfaces of Fe-9Mn-5Al-0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel processed by intercritical quenching-annealing at 620°C (QA-620, a), 680°C (QA-680, b), 740°C (QA-740, c), and 800°C (QA-800, d). Each subfigure reveals mixed fracture modes: cleavage regions (red dashed), quasi-cleavage regions (blue dashed), and dimpled ductile regions (yellow dashed), accompanied by second cracks, river patterns, cleavage steps, and laminar tears (red arrows). The QA-620 specimen exhibits significantly smaller micro-dimples and pronounced cleavage features within δ-ferrite. At 680°C and 740°C, cleavage/quasi-cleavage and dimpled areas lose distinct boundaries and interweave, with larger dimples (yellow arrows) becoming evident. QA-800 displays alternating cleavage layers rich in cleavage steps and dimple layers. These morphologies demonstrate that cracks initiate at δ-ferrite/tempered martensite interfaces due to stress concentration, propagate along soft δ-ferrite bands producing cleavage, but are blunted by adjacent tough regions generating dimples, with increasing annealing temperature enhancing ductile fracture characteristics.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization results for Fe-9Mn-5Al-0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel under hot-rolled (HR), intercritical quenching at 1050°C (IQ-1050), and quenching-annealing at 620°C, 680°C, 740°C, and 800°C (QA-620/680/740/800) conditions. Panels (a–f) show band contrast (BC) maps focusing on tempered martensite regions, revealing laminated microstructures composed of δ-ferrite bands and martensitic/austenitic laths. Panels (a-1–f-1) display corresponding kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps indicating geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) density distributions, where HR exhibits the highest GND density and IQ-1050 shows substantially reduced dislocation content. Panels (a-2–f-2) present grain boundary distribution maps analyzed via a Python-based crystallographic identification method, with black, blue, cyan, red, and green lines representing austenitic grain boundaries (15°–45°), high-angle packet boundaries, low-angle packet boundaries, block boundaries (>45°), and sub-block boundaries, respectively. Key trends include a continuous decrease in block boundary density with increasing annealing temperature, an initial increase followed by a decrease in austenitic grain boundary density at 800°C due to austenite coarsening, and a correlation between AG boundary density and austenite volume fraction. The reduction in high-angle block boundaries contributes to the decreased yield strength in annealed specimens, while IQ-1050 shows higher block boundary density than AG boundary density, contrasting with HR and indicating more extensive martensitic transformation.

This figure presents quantitative microstructure statistics derived from EBSD analysis for a low-density Fe–9Mn–5Al–0.3C δ-TRIP steel processed under different conditions: hot-rolled (HR), intercritically quenched at 1050 °C (IQ-1050), and quenching-annealed at 620 °C, 680 °C, 740 °C, and 800 °C (QA-620 to QA-800). Subfigure (a) plots the geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) density for the BCC phase (black squares) and FCC phase (red circles). The GND density monotonically decreases from HR toward higher annealing temperatures in both phases, with the BCC phase consistently showing higher values (from 10.81 to 4.63 × 10^14 m^-2) than the FCC phase (from 8.37 to 2.66 × 10^14 m^-2), reflecting progressive recovery and stress relief during tempering. Subfigure (b) shows the boundary density distribution across five crystallographic boundary categories: austenitic grain (AG) boundaries (15°–45°), high-angle packet boundaries, low-angle packet boundaries, block boundaries (>45°), and sub-block boundaries. The caption and associated text note that AG boundary density rises with annealing temperature but drops at 800 °C due to austenite grain coarsening, whereas block boundary density continuously decreases. The loss of block boundaries and the replacement by AG boundaries as reverted austenite grows are linked to the decline in yield strength of the annealed specimens.

Figure 9 presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization and derived austenite stability analysis for Fe-9Mn-5Al-0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel plates subjected to quenching-annealing (QA) at 620°C, 680°C, 740°C, and 800°C. Panel (a) displays XRD profiles before and after tensile testing, with diffraction peaks indexed as austenite (γ): (111)γ, (200)γ, (220)γ, (311)γ, (222)γ; and ferrite/martensite (α): (110)α, (200)α, (220)α, (211)α, (220)α, revealing diminished γ peak intensities after deformation. Panel (b) shows austenitic volume fractions determined from these profiles, where retained austenite decreases after tensile testing across all specimens; QA-740 exhibits the highest initial fraction near 51%, while QA-620 shows the lowest near 33%. Panel (c) plots the austenitic stability constant k (left axis) and stacking fault energy SFE (right axis, mJ/m²) versus annealing temperature, with QA-680 having the highest SFE of 38.27 mJ/m² and the lowest k of 0.56, indicating the highest austenite stability and minimal TRIP effect, whereas QA-740 and QA-800 possess higher k values (~1.47 and ~1.50) and lower SFEs (~33.29 and ~31.53 mJ/m²), signifying pronounced strain-induced martensitic transformation. The opposing trends of k and SFE with temperature provide consistent indicators of austenitic mechanical stability, supporting that both TRIP and TWIP effects operate during deformation.

Figure 10 presents the work hardening rate (WHR, red lines) and true stress-strain curves (black lines) for Fe-9Mn-5Al-0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel specimens processed by intercritical quenching-annealing at 620 °C (QA-620, a), 680 °C (QA-680, b), 740 °C (QA-740, c), and 800 °C (QA-800, d), with the necking point indicated by a yellow dot in each panel. All WHR curves show a analogous three-stage shape: Stage I exhibits a sharp decrease at ε < ~0.01 due to dislocation glide in soft δ-ferrite with large mean free path; Stage II (~0.01–0.15) shows a markedly slowed decline attributed to competing mechanisms including grain boundary hindrance, dislocation tangling, and the TRIP effect; Stage III (>0.15) displays a gradual WHR increase dominated by sustained TRIP and possible TWIP effects. QA-740 exhibits the most delayed necking point near 0.5 true strain, corresponding to the highest uniform elongation, whereas QA-680 necks earliest at approximately 0.25 strain. These comparisons demonstrate that annealing temperature critically governs austenite stability and deformation mechanisms, with the QA-740 condition achieving optimal work hardening capacity and ductility.

Figure 11 presents four histograms (a–d) illustrating austenitic grain size statistics derived from EBSD for Fe-9Mn-5Al-0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel prior to tensile testing. Subplots correspond to specimens processed by intercritical quenching-annealing at 620 °C (QA-620), 680 °C (QA-680), 740 °C (QA-740), and 800 °C (QA-800). The horizontal axis partitions equivalent circle diameter into bins from <1.6 μm to >4.8 μm, while the vertical axis gives the proportion of austenite grains in percent. All distributions share a consistent trend: the fraction is highest in the smallest size intervals and monotonically declines toward larger diameters. The caption states that roughly 90 % of grains are smaller than 4.8 μm. QA-620 and QA-680 show pronounced peaks in the finest classes, whereas QA-740 and QA-800 retain slightly higher fractions in the coarsest bin. These data underpin the argument that finer austenite grains exhibit higher alloy enrichment and stability, causing their martensitic transformation to occur only at greater critical strains and thereby prolonging the TRIP effect that drives sustained work hardening in stage III.
